Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Perfective clause (auxiliary 'don' + verb phrase), declarative fixed expression.
Figurative Meaning: A situation has gone beyond control or is no longer manageable.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: The situation has become too serious to fix easily.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech, politics, crisis discussion, family disputes.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Derived from food culture where excess water spoils gari preparation; common nationwide.
First Example Sentence: By di time dem call police, wata don pass gari.
Second Example Sentence: If you no pay rent early, wata don pass gari.
Third Example Sentence: As scandal leak online, wata don pass gari completely.

Idiom: Monkey no fine but e mama like am.
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Compound declarative clause with contrastive conjunction.
Figurative Meaning: People naturally defend or love their own despite flaws.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: Parents or supporters overlook faults of their own.
Usage Contexts: Family talk, humor, politics, social commentary.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Animal imagery common in West African oral tradition.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA): /ˈmɔŋki no faɪn bɔt i ˈmama laɪk am/
First Example Sentence: You fit criticize am, but monkey no fine but e mama like am.
Second Example Sentence: Even with all e mistake, monkey no fine but e mama like am.
Third Example Sentence: Supporters stand firm because monkey no fine but e mama like am.

Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: To steal public or entrusted money.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Metaphorical extension of 'chop' (eat) meaning to consume resources.
First Example Sentence: Dem accuse di treasurer say e chop money.
Second Example Sentence: If you chop money, audit go catch you.
Third Example Sentence: People vex because officials dey chop money.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase functioning as idiomatic predicate.
Figurative Meaning: To be left behind or to fail to keep up.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in school ranking culture across Cameroon.
First Example Sentence: If you no read, you go carry last for class.
Second Example Sentence: Run fast no make you carry last.
Third Example Sentence: Company no wan carry last for technology race.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Imagery of careful walking on rough terrain.
First Example Sentence: As you enter new market, put eye for ground.
Second Example Sentence: Election time, citizens for put eye for ground.
Third Example Sentence: If you travel night, put eye for ground well.

Idiom: Fire dey burn for e heart.
Figurative Meaning: To feel intense anger or jealousy.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent: He feels very angry or jealous.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Fire metaphor common in oral storytelling.
First Example Sentence: When e see di success, fire dey burn for e heart.
Second Example Sentence: Fire dey burn for e heart because dem ignore am.
Third Example Sentence: Anytime topic come up, fire dey burn for e heart again.

Figurative Meaning: To prepare seriously for hardship or challenge.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reference to traditional wrapper clothing worn securely before work.
First Example Sentence: As fuel price rise, make everybody tie wrapper tight.
Second Example Sentence: Exam season come, tie wrapper tight.
Third Example Sentence: If business hard, tie wrapper tight and push forward.

First Example Sentence: Dis kind excuse na small pikin play.
Second Example Sentence: You still dey quarrel over dat? Na small pikin play.
Third Example Sentence: Manager say dat strategy na small pikin play.

Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reference to minimal bedding in modest households.
First Example Sentence: Before success, e bin dey sleep for mat.
Second Example Sentence: Plenty youth dey sleep for mat before dem breakthrough.
Third Example Sentence: Story show how hero sleep for mat then rise.

Figurative Meaning: To retaliate or prove superiority strongly.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Pepper symbolizes heat and intensity in local cuisine.
First Example Sentence: Team promise say dem go show pepper for final.
Second Example Sentence: After insult, she show pepper sharp.
Third Example Sentence: Company show pepper with new innovation.
